source: Invest Like the Best — Tech and consumer growth investing (EP.149)
summary: Data quantity is the single most predictive variable of AI quality, creating compounding moats for dominant-scale internet platforms.
summary_extended: Baker argues that data quantity is the single most predictive variable of AI quality, citing multiple Google and Microsoft Research papers showing that every order-of-magnitude increase in training data doubles AI quality (measured as halving error rates). This creates a powerful self-reinforcing feedback loop: dominant market share generates the most users, who generate the most data, which produces the fastest-improving AI algorithms, which in turn attracts more users — compounding the moat over time. Baker presents this as a core structural view on why mean reversion is unlikely for dominant-scale internet platforms, as the data advantage of incumbents is nearly impossible for challengers to replicate. The investment implication is to own the largest-scale internet platforms with the most user-generated data, as their AI quality advantage will widen structurally over the long term.
detail: Per multiple Google and Microsoft Research papers, every order of magnitude increase in training data doubles AI quality (measured as halving error rate). This means dominant market share → most users → most data → fastest-improving algorithms, creating a self-reinforcing feedback loop that entrenches incumbents and reduces mean reversion.
confidence_evidence: Backed by specific academic citations; Baker presents this as a core structural view on competitive dynamics.
quote_fragment: for every order of magnitude increase in data, the quality of the AI doubles

ticker_audit.reasoning: Google is the canonical example Baker cites — its research papers are the source of the data-scaling law, and it is the dominant-scale internet platform with the largest data moat across Search, YouTube, Maps, and Android.

Meta operates at massive scale with billions of users generating social and behavioral data across Facebook, Instagram, and WhatsApp, making it a direct beneficiary of the data-quantity-drives-AI-quality thesis. [RE-AUDIT: While Meta is an obvious beneficiary of the data-quantity thesis, Baker did not specifically name Facebook/Meta in connection with this exact structural claim in the transcript. It appears to have been included by inference rather than explicit connection.]

Amazon operates dominant-scale platforms in e-commerce and cloud (AWS), generating enormous quantities of behavioral and transactional data that directly benefit from the data-scaling dynamic Baker describes. [RE-AUDIT: Amazon is a plausible beneficiary of the data-scaling thesis, but Baker did not specifically name Amazon in connection with this exact claim. Its inclusion appears inferential rather than based on Baker explicitly linking it to this thesis.]

Microsoft Research is explicitly cited as a source of the scaling papers, and Microsoft operates large-scale platforms (LinkedIn, Bing, Azure, Office 365) generating substantial user data, making it a direct beneficiary of this structural thesis.
